questions.stem: A 72-year-old woman presents with a 6-month history of difficulty walking, described as 'her feet getting stuck to the floor.' Her family reports she has become forgetful and apathetic over the same period. She also has urgency of urine but no dysuria or incontinence. Neurological examination reveals a broad-based, shuffling gait with difficulty initiating steps. Lower limb power is normal, but there is gegenhalten rigidity. CT head shows ventriculomegaly without cortical atrophy. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The classic triad of gait apraxia (feet 'stuck to floor'), cognitive decline, and urinary urgency, combined with ventriculomegaly out of proportion to atrophy on CT, is highly characteristic of normal pressure hydrocephalus. Alzheimer's typically spares gait early, and Parkinson's would show tremor, rigidity, and bradykinesia with a narrower base.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man presents with progressive gait difficulty over 8 months. He has a magnetic gait with difficulty turning, but no tremor or rigidity. His Mini-Mental State Examination score is 28/30 (normal). He denies urinary symptoms. MRI brain shows ventriculomegaly with periventricular hyperintensities and no significant cortical atrophy. Which condition is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Despite the absence of urinary symptoms and mild cognitive changes, the gait apraxia with ventriculomegaly out of proportion to atrophy points to normal pressure hydrocephalus (NPH can present with gait only in early stages). Vascular parkinsonism typically has lower-body parkinsonism with diffuse white matter disease but not isolated ventriculomegaly; Parkinson's disease has tremor/rigidity; cervical myelopathy would show upper motor neuron signs in legs.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man who had a subarachnoid haemorrhage from a ruptured anterior communicating artery aneurysm 4 months ago presents with worsening gait unsteadiness, mild confusion, and urinary urgency. His Glasgow Coma Scale is 15. CT head shows enlargement of the lateral ventricles, third ventricle, and fourth ventricle with no obstruction. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Post-subarachnoid haemorrhage communicating hydrocephalus is a classic cause of normal pressure hydrocephalus, presenting with the triad and ventriculomegaly without obstruction. Hydrocephalus ex vacuo would show commensurate cortical atrophy, not post-haemorrhagic. Chronic subdural haematoma would cause midline shift and a concave lens shape on CT; cerebral oedema is acute; ventriculitis would have signs of infection.

questions.stem: A 74-year-old woman presents with progressive memory loss over 2 years, now with difficulty walking and occasional urinary incontinence. She has a broad-based gait with short steps. MRI brain shows ventriculomegaly and moderate medial temporal lobe atrophy. Which is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The presence of significant medial temporal lobe atrophy on MRI strongly favours Alzheimer's disease over NPH. In NPH, ventriculomegaly occurs out of proportion to cortical atrophy; here, atrophy is prominent, making Alzheimer's more likely despite the gait and bladder symptoms (which can occur late in Alzheimer's). Lewy body dementia would have parkinsonism, hallucinations, and fluctuation; vascular dementia would have stepwise decline and focal signs.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man presents with a 1-year history of slowly progressive gait difficulty and mild short-term memory problems. He has no urinary symptoms. Examination shows a wide-based, cautious gait with mild rigidity in all limbs and a positive 'frontal release' sign (grasp reflex). MRI brain shows enlargement of the lateral, third, and fourth ventricles with no obstruction, and no significant cortical atrophy. CSF opening pressure is 15 cmH2O. Which is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The combination of classic gait apraxia, mild cognitive decline, ventriculomegaly without obstruction, and normal CSF pressure is diagnostic of normal pressure hydrocephalus. Aqueduct stenosis would cause enlargement of lateral/third ventricles but not fourth; Chiari malformation has hindbrain herniation; idiopathic intracranial hypertension has elevated pressure (>25 cmH2O) and often headache/visual symptoms; cerebral atrophy would show cortical thinning.

questions.stem: A 76-year-old man presents with a 6-month history of unsteady gait and recent memory lapses. He also reports occasional urinary urgency. Neurological exam shows a magnetic gait and mild cognitive impairment. What is the best initial diagnostic investigation?
questions.explanation: MRI brain is the preferred initial test as it provides detailed visualization of ventricular size, 'tight high convexity' sign, and excludes other causes. CT is less sensitive and LP is not first-line.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old woman with suspected normal pressure hydrocephalus undergoes an MRI which shows enlarged ventricles and periventricular white matter changes, but the cortical sulci are also prominent. What additional MRI feature would support a diagnosis of NPH over cerebral atrophy?
options: Disproportionately enlarged subarachnoid space hydrocephalus (DESH) pattern
questions.explanation: DESH pattern—tight high convexity sulci with enlarged ventricles—is characteristic of NPH, whereas global atrophy shows prominent sulci throughout. Other options are not specific.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man has a clinical triad suggestive of NPH. MRI shows ventriculomegaly but with some cortical atrophy. A lumbar puncture reveals opening pressure of 18 cm H2O and normal CSF analysis. What is the most appropriate next investigation to predict response to shunt surgery?
questions.explanation: The CSF tap test (removal of 40-50 mL) with gait assessment is the best predictor of shunt response. Cisternography is less specific and used less commonly.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old with a history of subarachnoid haemorrhage 3 months ago now presents with progressive gait difficulty and cognitive decline. What diagnostic finding on MRI is most consistent with communicating hydrocephalus?
options: Enlarged lateral ventricles with periventricular ooze
Ventriculomegaly out of proportion to sulcal atrophy
questions.explanation: Communicating hydrocephalus shows ventricular enlargement without corresponding sulcal atrophy. Transependymal oedema suggests acute high-pressure hydrocephalus, not NPH.

questions.stem: A 78-year-old woman with known Alzheimer's disease develops a shuffling gait and urinary incontinence. MRI shows moderate ventriculomegaly and cortical atrophy. What single best test confirms that her symptoms are due to superimposed NPH rather than advanced Alzheimer's?
options: Gait assessment after large-volume lumbar puncture
questions.explanation: Improvement in gait after removal of 40-50 mL CSF (tap test) strongly supports NPH as a contributing factor. CSF biomarkers diagnose Alzheimer's but do not exclude NPH.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man presents with a 6-month history of progressive gait difficulty, cognitive decline, and urinary urgency. MRI brain shows ventriculomegaly out of proportion to cortical atrophy, with no obstruction. A lumbar puncture shows normal opening pressure and removal of 40 mL of CSF improves his gait speed. What is the most appropriate first-line definitive treatment?
options: Regular therapeutic lumbar punctures every 2 weeks
questions.explanation: The definitive treatment for idiopathic NPH is CSF diversion via a shunt. Ventriculoperitoneal shunting is the most common first-line procedure. Endoscopic third ventriculostomy is reserved for obstructive hydrocephalus. Acetazolamide and repeated LPs provide only temporary or no benefit.

questions.stem: A 75-year-old woman with a 1-year history of typical normal pressure hydrocephalus (NPH) has been assessed and confirmed to have communicating hydrocephalus with a positive CSF drainage test. She has a history of atrial fibrillation and is awaiting shunt surgery. Which treatment is contraindicated in this patient's management?
questions.explanation: Endoscopic third ventriculostomy is contraindicated in communicating hydrocephalus such as NPH; it is only effective for obstructive hydrocephalus. The other options are either indicated or not contraindicated.

questions.stem: An 82-year-old man with known NPH has severe dementia (MMSE 8/30) and is bedbound. His family asks about definitive treatment. Which is the most appropriate first-line management?
options: Conservative management including best supportive care
questions.explanation: In patients with severe dementia and poor functional status, the risks of shunt surgery often outweigh the benefits. NICE recommends considering shunt surgery only in patients likely to benefit; conservative management is appropriate here.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with iNPH is being evaluated for shunt surgery. He has a large irreducible inguinal hernia and a history of multiple abdominal surgeries with dense adhesions. What is the most appropriate definitive treatment?
questions.explanation: Ventriculoperitoneal shunt is contraindicated due to abdominal pathology. Ventriculoatrial shunt is an alternative distal site that avoids the peritoneal cavity. Endoscopic third ventriculostomy is not indicated for communicating hydrocephalus. Lumboperitoneal shunt still uses the peritoneal space and would be similarly contraindicated.

questions.stem: A 79-year-old woman with iNPH is deemed a good candidate for CSF diversion but is unable to tolerate general anaesthesia due to severe COPD. What is the most appropriate definitive treatment?
options: Endoscopic third ventriculostomy under local anaesthesia
questions.explanation: Lumboperitoneal shunts can be inserted under local anaesthesia, making them suitable for patients who cannot tolerate GA. Ventriculoperitoneal shunts typically require GA. Endoscopic third ventriculostomy is not indicated for communicating hydrocephalus, and therapeutic LPs are not definitive.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old woman is referred by her GP with a 6-month history of progressive gait difficulty, memory problems, and occasional urinary incontinence. She has no headache. On examination, she has a broad-based, shuffling gait with difficulty turning. Mini-Mental State Examination score is 24/30. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Refer to neurosurgery for ventriculoperitoneal shunt insertion
Perform a lumbar puncture with CSF pressure measurement
Start a trial of oral acetazolamide
questions.explanation: The classic triad of gait apraxia, cognitive decline, and urinary incontinence raises suspicion for normal pressure hydrocephalus. NICE recommends CT head as the first-line imaging to assess ventricular size and exclude other causes such as atrophy or mass lesions. Shunt insertion is only considered after further supportive tests.

questions.stem: A 74-year-old man with suspected idiopathic normal pressure hydrocephalus has undergone clinical assessment, CT head showing ventriculomegaly with no cortical atrophy, and a lumbar puncture with opening pressure of 14 cmH₂O. His gait disturbance continues to worsen. What is the most appropriate next step in his management?
options: Arrange a trial of continuous external lumbar drainage
Start a course of oral acetazolamide
Arrange insertion of a ventriculoperitoneal shunt
Repeat the CT head in 6 months
questions.explanation: Before permanent shunt insertion, a trial of controlled CSF drainage (e.g., 3-day external lumbar drainage) is recommended to assess the likelihood of improvement. Shunt surgery is only offered if there is a clear response to CSF drainage.

questions.stem: A 78-year-old woman with a ventriculoperitoneal shunt inserted for normal pressure hydrocephalus 10 days ago presents to the emergency department with sudden severe headache, nausea, and vomiting. Her GCS is 14 (E4 V4 M6). Temperature is 37.2°C. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Check the shunt valve setting by palpation
Arrange an urgent non-contrast CT head
questions.explanation: In a patient with a recent shunt and acute headache with vomiting, the priority is to exclude shunt obstruction, overdrainage (e.g., subdural haematoma), or infection. Urgent CT head is the first investigation to identify the cause. Neurosurgical advice can be sought concurrently, but imaging is the key immediate action.

questions.stem: An 82-year-old man with a history of normal pressure hydrocephalus was assessed 6 months ago. CT head then showed ventriculomegaly with no significant atrophy, but he was deemed too frail for shunt surgery due to multiple comorbidities. He now presents with worsening gait and cognition, and his daughter reports two falls in the past month. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Perform a lumbar puncture and remove 40-50 mL of CSF
Refer to the falls clinic for multifactorial assessment
Arrange a repeat non-contrast CT head
questions.explanation: Given the change in symptoms, a repeat CT head is essential to rule out new pathology such as interval progression of hydrocephalus, a subdural haematoma (especially if he is on antiplatelets or anticoagulants), or alternative causes. Lumbar puncture is not the first step without recent imaging, and other options do not address the acute change.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with idiopathic normal pressure hydrocephalus underwent insertion of a ventriculoperitoneal shunt 3 months ago. He initially improved but has now developed new confusion and has had two falls in the past week. He has no headache or fever. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Request a repeat mini-mental state examination
Perform a shunt tap to measure intracranial pressure
Refer to occupational therapy for home assessment
Start a short course of oral dexamethasone
questions.explanation: New neurological symptoms after shunting, especially falls and confusion, raise concern for a subdural haematoma due to overdrainage. CT head is the first-line investigation to detect this complication before considering shunt tap or other interventions.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man presents with a 6-month history of progressive gait difficulty, described as 'magnetic' and unsteady. His wife reports he has become forgetful over the same period. In the clinic, he is alert but struggles to recall recent events. His gait is broad-based and short-stepped. Bladder function is normal. Which single feature would make you most concerned that this is NOT normal pressure hydrocephalus but a more urgent mimic?
options: Sudden onset of symptoms over hours
questions.explanation: Sudden onset of symptoms (over hours) suggests a vascular cause such as multiple lacunar strokes or a strategic infarct, rather than the insidious onset typical of NPH. The classic triad of NPH (gait apraxia, cognitive decline, urinary incontinence) develops over months. Hallucinations, rest tremor, and marked fluctuations are more characteristic of Lewy body dementia or Parkinson's disease, but are not acute emergencies. Sudden onset is a red flag requiring urgent neuroimaging.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old woman with known normal pressure hydrocephalus (diagnosed 2 years ago, treated with VP shunt) attends the emergency department with a 1-day history of severe headache, vomiting, and drowsiness. Her observations are: GCS 13, BP 145/85, HR 55, temperature 37.1°C. She has a palpable shunt valve over the right parietal region. What single finding on examination would most suggest life-threatening raised intracranial pressure due to shunt obstruction?
questions.explanation: A unilateral dilated fixed pupil (third nerve palsy) is a sign of uncal herniation due to raised ICP, which can occur with acute shunt obstruction. This is a surgical emergency requiring urgent neurosurgical review. Papilloedema may be absent in early raised ICP; nuchal rigidity suggests meningitis; decerebrate posturing is a late sign of diffuse brainstem dysfunction; absent doll's eye indicates brainstem death. The fixed dilated pupil is the most localizing and immediately concerning sign of herniation.

questions.stem: A 75-year-old man with idiopathic normal pressure hydrocephalus (iNPH) is referred to the falls clinic. He has the classic triad: gait apraxia, cognitive impairment, and urge incontinence. He is awaiting shunt surgery. Today, he reports no headache, no vomiting, and no new focal weakness. His gait is slow but he can walk unaided. Which single finding on examination would most warrant immediate admission and urgent CT head?
options: Cogwheel rigidity in the right arm
questions.explanation: Papilloedema indicates raised intracranial pressure, which is NOT a feature of untreated iNPH (CSF pressure is normal). Its presence suggests a different pathology (e.g., idiopathic intracranial hypertension, tumour, venous sinus thrombosis) or a complication such as shunt-triggered subdural haematoma. This requires urgent neuroimaging. Bilateral upgoing plantars can occur with NPH due to periventricular white matter damage; grasp reflexes are frontal release signs; cogwheel rigidity suggests parkinsonism; 'stuck' gait is typical of NPH. None are emergency findings per se.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old woman with a VP shunt for normal pressure hydrocephalus presents with a 2-week history of progressive lethargy, low-grade fever, and mild headache. On examination, she is febrile (38.2°C), GCS 15, has no focal signs, but there is mild nuchal rigidity. Shunt site is non-tender. She has a history of recurrent urinary tract infections. What is the single most important diagnosis to exclude IMMEDIATELY?
options: Bacterial meningitis secondary to shunt infection
Infection of the distal shunt tubing
questions.explanation: Bacterial meningitis secondary to shunt infection is a life-threatening complication that must be excluded with CSF culture. Symptoms of lethargy, fever, and nuchal rigidity in a shunted patient are a red flag for CNS infection. While UTI is common, it does not explain the nuchal rigidity. Shunt nephritis is rare and presents later. Distal shunt infection may cause abdominal pain but not meningitis. Shunt malfunction without infection would not cause fever or nuchal rigidity. CSF sampling is urgent.

questions.stem: A 78-year-old man with normal pressure hydrocephalus (untreated) is brought to the emergency department by his son because of a fall at home. He has a known history of hypertension and type 2 diabetes. On examination, GCS 15, pupils equal and reactive, no focal neurological deficit. He has a bruised hip but no obvious fracture. His gait is cautious and broad-based. Which single finding on history or examination would most suggest an acute intracranial pathology mimicking NPH?
options: He reports seeing 'little green men' in the house
Long-standing urinary incontinence that is worse today
Worsening of gait over the last 3 days after the fall
He is unable to remember 3 objects at 5 minutes
He has a resting tremor in the left hand
questions.explanation: Acute worsening of gait after a fall raises concern for a subdural haematoma, which can present with gait disturbance and cognitive decline, mimicking NPH. In an elderly patient on antiplatelets/anticoagulants (not mentioned but common), this is a can't-miss diagnosis. Worsening of gait over days is not typical of NPH (slow progression over months). Urinary incontinence worsening is non-specific; hallucinations suggest Lewy body dementia; memory impairment is typical; resting tremor suggests Parkinson's. The acute change post-fall is the red flag.
